subsystem: ImageMagick CALS Type 1 raster image reader. ReadCALSImage() is a coder entry point (coders/cals.c) that parses the fixed 2048-byte CALS record header (sixteen 128-byte records) out of an untrusted image blob, extracts document metadata (density, pixel counts / width+height, orientation, type), spools the remaining Group 4 (CCITT T.6) bitmap into a temporary file, and then re-invokes the pipeline with the 'group4:' coder to actually decode the image.
provenance: Identified by ImageMagick core API and idioms: MaxTextExtent, MagickCoreSignature, MagickBooleanType/MagickFalse, AcquireImage/OpenBlob/ReadBlob/ReadBlobByte/CloseBlob/DestroyImage, LogMagickEvent(TraceEvent,GetMagickModule()), AcquireUniqueFileResource/RelinquishUniqueFileResource, CloneImageInfo/SetImageInfoBlob/DestroyImageInfo, FormatLocaleString/ConstantString/CopyMagickString, ThrowImageException, OrientationType, and ReadImage. The banner names the file corpus/train/train_0172_cwe252.c and the sample tag /* sample: primevul_c_train_2502_fixed */; the 'CALS' magick string at line 140 and the 'rdensty:/rpelcnt:/rorient:/rtype:' record keys confirm this is the CALS Type 1 raster coder.
trust_boundary: The entire image file is attacker-controlled: it arrives via OpenBlob and is consumed byte-for-byte through ReadBlob/ReadBlobByte. Every field parsed out of the header records is hostile — the 128-byte record contents, the ASCII decimal numbers fed to sscanf (density, width, height, pel_path, direction, type), and the trailing Group 4 bitmap bytes spooled to the temp file. image_info->filename and the exception object are trusted (caller-supplied), guarded by asserts. The parsed width/height/density strings cross a second trust boundary when they are handed to the downstream group4 decoder as the image geometry.

what_the_code_does.code:
static Image *ReadCALSImage(const ImageInfo *image_info,
  ExceptionInfo *exception)
{
  char
    filename[MaxTextExtent],
    header[129],
    message[MaxTextExtent];
what_the_code_does.explanation: Coder entry point matching ImageMagick's Read<FORMAT>Image signature: it receives the trusted ImageInfo (open options, source filename) and an ExceptionInfo out-parameter, and returns a newly-decoded Image* (or NULL). Three stack buffers are declared: filename[MaxTextExtent] (holds the path of a spooled temporary file), header[129] (holds one CALS record — deliberately one byte larger than the 128-byte record so a trailing NUL can guarantee C-string termination), and message[MaxTextExtent] used to format the geometry/density strings. MaxTextExtent is ImageMagick's standard fixed buffer size (4096).

Local state: file is the stdio handle for the temp spool file; image is the working Image object; read_info is a cloned ImageInfo used to re-enter the pipeline for the actual Group 4 decode; c holds each spooled byte and unique_file the temp-file descriptor; status carries OpenBlob's result; i is the record loop counter. The seven unsigned long metadata values (density, direction, height, orientation, pel_path, type, width) are all populated directly from attacker-controlled header text via sscanf, so each is a hostile 64-bit (on LP64) integer once parsed.

Standard coder preamble: asserts validate the trusted caller structs and their signature words (debug-build sanity checks), optional trace logging, then AcquireImage allocates the Image and OpenBlob attaches the untrusted input stream in binary read mode. If OpenBlob fails, the image is torn down with DestroyImageList and NULL is returned — this is the correct error path and cleans up the allocation. Note AcquireImage's return is used without an explicit NULL check, but AcquireImage aborts internally rather than returning NULL, so that is idiomatic.

The header buffer is zero-filled ONCE before the loop, which is what guarantees header[128] stays NUL for the whole function (ReadBlob only ever writes indices 0..127), keeping the buffer a valid C string for LocaleNCompare/sscanf. All metadata is initialized to safe defaults (orientation=1 meaning TopLeft, type=1, geometry 0). These defaults are important: because the sscanf return values are ignored later, a field that fails to parse simply retains the default set here.

The CALS Type 1 header is sixteen fixed 128-byte records. The loop reads exactly one record per iteration into header[0..127]. Critically, ReadBlob's return is CHECKED: if fewer than 128 bytes are available the loop breaks rather than parsing a short/stale record — this is the corrected CWE-252 behavior (an unchecked read here would parse leftover bytes from the previous record). The switch dispatches on *header (the record's first byte); only records beginning with 'R'/'r' carry the keyword fields this coder cares about.

For records starting with R/r, the code matches keyword prefixes with LocaleNCompare (bounded case-insensitive compare of a fixed count). The 'rdensty:' record's numeric tail begins at header+8; sscanf with "%lu" parses the scanner density into the unsigned long density. Because header is NUL-terminated at index 128, sscanf cannot run off the buffer. The sscanf return value is discarded via (void).

The 'rpelcnt:' record carries the pixel counts as two comma-separated decimal integers, parsed into width and height. These two values are the security-load-bearing outputs of this function: they later become the image geometry string handed to the Group 4 decoder. There is no range validation on either value — any decimal up to ULONG_MAX is accepted — and the sscanf result is ignored, so a missing second number leaves height at its default 0.

The 'rorient:' record supplies pel_path and direction rotation angles. The code maps recognized pel_path angles (90/180/270) to EXIF-style orientation codes (5/3/7), leaving orientation at 1 for 0/unrecognized, and bumps orientation by one when direction is 90. The resulting orientation is later cast to OrientationType. Values outside {90,180,270} silently leave the default TopLeft, and unparsed input leaves both angles at 0.

The 'rtype:' record (6-char prefix) yields the raster type into type via sscanf at header+6. The final inner break handles any other R/r record, and the closing braces end the switch, the for-loop, and the record-parsing phase. Note type is parsed but never subsequently used in the shown code — it is effectively dead, though harmless.

A unique temporary file path is reserved into filename and its descriptor opened, then wrapped with fdopen for buffered writing. If the reservation or fdopen fails, ThrowImageException raises the FileOpenError. In ImageMagick this macro records the exception and returns NULL from the reader — but at this point the working image is still open (OpenBlob'd) and, if unique_file succeeded but fdopen failed, the reserved temp file resource is still held, so this error path is a potential cleanup gap.

The remainder of the untrusted blob (the Group 4 encoded bitmap) is copied byte-by-byte to the temp file; the loop stops on EOF or a write error. Then the temp file is closed, the input blob closed, and the original Image destroyed. The fputc return is compared against c to detect short writes (disk-full/quota), which is good hygiene; fclose's return, however, is discarded, so a deferred write error on flush would be missed.

A fresh ImageInfo is cloned, its blob cleared, and its filename set to 'group4:<tempfile>' so the pipeline re-enters using the Group 4/CCITT decoder on the spooled bytes. The attacker-controlled width and height are formatted as a 'WxH' geometry string into read_info->size, density likewise into read_info->density, and orientation is stored. ReadImage then performs the real decode. This is where the parsed geometry crosses into the downstream decoder as authoritative image dimensions — the group4 reader will honor read_info->size.

If the Group 4 decode succeeded, the returned Image's identity fields are overwritten to reflect the CALS origin: filename and magick_filename are copied from the trusted source path with the bounded CopyMagickString, and the magick tag is set to 'CALS'. Finally read_info is freed and the temporary file resource released, then the decoded image (or NULL) is returned. CloneImageInfo's return is used without a NULL check, and RelinquishUniqueFileResource is only reached on this normal path (not on the earlier ThrowImageException path).

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This and the other three sscanf calls (lines 77, 82, 87, 102) explicitly discard the return value with (void). sscanf reports how many conversions succeeded; ignoring it means the code cannot tell a successful parse from a failed one. When the attacker supplies a record whose keyword matches but whose numeric tail is missing or non-numeric (e.g. 'rpelcnt:abc' or a truncated 'rpelcnt:'), the target variables (density/width/height/pel_path/direction/type) are NOT written and silently retain their loop-init defaults. This is the CWE-252 pattern (unchecked return of a value-producing call): behavior becomes controlled by whether initialization happens to be safe rather than by validated parsing. Here the pre-init at lines 59-65 makes it non-fatal, but the invariant 'act only on values sscanf actually produced' is not enforced — the implementation should check the conversion count.

width and height are parsed as unbounded unsigned long with no upper limit, then at line 128 formatted into read_info->size ('%lux%lu') and passed to the Group 4 decoder via ReadImage. An attacker can declare an enormous geometry (e.g. 'rpelcnt:4294967295,4294967295'). The downstream CCITT/Group 4 reader may honor read_info->size and attempt to allocate width*height-scale pixel storage, and width*height on the consuming side is a classic multiplication that can integer-overflow (CWE-190) or drive an unbounded allocation (CWE-789 / CWE-20 memory-exhaustion DoS). The CALS coder performs no sanity check that the declared pixel counts are consistent with the actual amount of Group 4 data spooled. Invariant the implementation must uphold: validate/clamp declared dimensions before handing them to a decoder that trusts them for allocation.

On the fdopen-failure sub-case (unique_file != -1 but fdopen returned NULL), the raw descriptor unique_file / reserved temp resource is leaked: ThrowImageException exits the reader without calling close(unique_file) or RelinquishUniqueFileResource(filename), and the still-open input Image is not destroyed on this path either. Repeatedly feeding inputs that trip this path (or the resource-exhaustion condition itself) leaks file descriptors and temp-file resources (CWE-772/CWE-404). Invariant: every early-exit must release the descriptor, the unique-file reservation, and the working Image. Confirm ThrowImageException's expansion in this build actually returns and does not itself clean these up.

CloneImageInfo's return is used immediately by SetImageInfoBlob and later dereferenced (read_info->filename, read_info->size, read_info->density, read_info->orientation) with no NULL guard. If CloneImageInfo could return NULL under allocation pressure, this becomes a NULL-pointer dereference (CWE-476). In stock ImageMagick CloneImageInfo aborts internally on OOM rather than returning NULL, so this is latent/low, but it is an unchecked return that the review must flag per the method — confirm the library contract for the version in use.

The temp-file spool is written with buffered stdio (fdopen 'wb' at line 115, fputc at line 119) but fclose's return is discarded. A short/failed final flush (disk full, quota) after fputc appeared to succeed produces a truncated Group 4 stream that is then decoded as if complete. This is a second CWE-252 instance (ignored close/flush status) whose impact is a corrupted/partial decode rather than memory corruption. Invariant: check fclose (and ideally fflush) so a write failure aborts rather than silently truncating the spooled bitmap.

Worth stating explicitly because it is the obvious overflow candidate and it is SAFE here: header is 129 bytes, ReadBlob at line 68 writes exactly 128 bytes into indices 0..127, and the one-time memset at line 58 leaves header[128]=0, so the buffer is always NUL-terminated and neither the LocaleNCompare (count-bounded) nor the sscanf (%lu on a NUL-terminated string) can read out of bounds. No off-by-one exists. The only way this would break is if a future edit moved the memset inside... it is not. Recorded to document that the fixed-buffer-vs-read-length contract is correctly upheld.

summary: A CALS Type 1 raster reader that safely bounds its 128-byte-into-129-byte header reads (ReadBlob length is checked, buffer is NUL-terminated) but ignores every sscanf return value (CWE-252): parsed metadata defaults silently on malformed input. The materially exploitable concern is that attacker-declared, unvalidated width/height are passed as authoritative geometry to the downstream Group 4 decoder (potential integer overflow / unbounded allocation DoS). Secondary issues are resource/descriptor leaks on the temp-file error path and other unchecked return values. No memory-corruption bug is present in the shown code.

reviewed_anchors.reason: 128-byte ReadBlob into a 129-byte buffer with a one-time memset guarantees NUL termination; no OOB read/write. Safe contract, documented in walkthrough.
Written only by AcquireUniqueFileResource and read by bounded FormatLocaleString/RelinquishUniqueFileResource; not attacker-sized.
Only ever written via FormatLocaleString with the MaxTextExtent bound; no overflow.
Return IS checked; short read breaks the loop. This is the corrected CWE-252 read-length check.
Return value discarded (CWE-252); density retains default on parse failure.
Unchecked return AND unbounded width/height flow to downstream decoder as geometry (DoS / integer overflow risk).
Return discarded (CWE-252); folded into the sscanf finding — unrecognized/failed parse silently keeps default orientation.
Return discarded, but type is never used after parsing; no security effect.
Fixed 16-iteration cap over 128-byte reads; correctly bounded, no unbounded loop.
header[0] is valid after a confirmed 128-byte read; benign dispatch.
orientation is constrained to {1,3,5,7} (+1) by the code; narrowing cast is in range and only sets metadata.
fdopen-failure sub-path leaks the descriptor/temp reservation and does not destroy the working image (CWE-772/404).
Write return IS checked; short-write breaks the loop. Good hygiene.
Return discarded (CWE-252); a failed final flush truncates the Group 4 stream silently.
Return dereferenced without NULL check (latent CWE-476; library aborts on OOM in stock builds).
Return used without NULL check but AcquireImage aborts internally rather than returning NULL; idiomatic.
Return IS checked; failure path destroys the image and returns NULL correctly.
Bounded by MaxTextExtent; filename is a controlled temp path, not attacker text.
Conduit that carries unvalidated width/height into read_info->size; covered by the geometry finding.
All bounded by MaxTextExtent and copy trusted image_info->filename / a literal; no overflow.
Correct cleanup on the normal path; the leak is that it is skipped on the ThrowImageException path (see finding).

findings.title: Attacker-declared width/height passed unvalidated to Group 4 decoder as image geometry
findings.bug_class: Unvalidated image dimensions driving downstream allocation (integer overflow / memory exhaustion)
findings.where_it_lives: implementation (file not provided) — the group4/CCITT decoder reached via ReadImage(read_info,...)
findings.invariant: Declared pixel counts from the 'rpelcnt:' record must be range-checked/clamped and reconciled against the actual spooled Group 4 data before being used as authoritative geometry by a decoder that allocates based on them.
findings.failure_mode: A CALS file with 'rpelcnt:<huge>,<huge>' sets read_info->size to an arbitrarily large WxH string; the downstream Group 4 reader trusts it and may compute width*height (integer overflow) or attempt a very large allocation, yielding a crash or memory-exhaustion DoS with no corresponding pixel data.
findings.what_to_confirm: In the group4/CCITT decoder, confirm whether read_info->size is used to size any allocation and whether width*height is bounds-checked before multiplication.

findings.title: Unchecked sscanf return values on all parsed header fields (CWE-252)
findings.bug_class: Ignored return value of a value-producing call
findings.invariant: Code must act only on values that sscanf actually produced; the conversion count must be checked and the field must not be consumed if the conversion failed.
findings.failure_mode: A keyword-matching record with a missing/non-numeric tail (e.g. 'rpelcnt:' with no digits) leaves the target variable unwritten; execution proceeds on the loop-init default. Here defaults are pre-set (lines 59-65) so it is non-fatal, but correctness silently depends on that rather than on validated input, and any future removal of a default would become use-of-uninitialized.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the intended behavior is to reject records that fail to parse; if so, the (void) casts should become an if (sscanf(...) == N) guard.

findings.title: Descriptor / temp-file resource and image leak on fdopen error path
findings.bug_class: Resource leak on error path (fd + temp reservation + Image)
findings.invariant: Every early exit must release the acquired descriptor, the unique-file reservation, and destroy the still-open working Image before returning.
findings.failure_mode: If AcquireUniqueFileResource succeeds (unique_file != -1) but fdopen returns NULL, ThrowImageException exits the reader without close(unique_file), without RelinquishUniqueFileResource(filename), and without DestroyImage(image). Repeated triggering leaks file descriptors and temp-file slots, a slow DoS. The image opened at line 48 is also leaked on this path.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm ThrowImageException's macro expansion returns here without freeing these, and whether an fdopen failure with a valid fd is reachable; if so add close/relinquish/DestroyImage before throwing.

findings.title: Unchecked fclose return truncates spooled Group 4 stream silently
findings.invariant: A buffered write pipeline must verify fflush/fclose success so a deferred write error aborts the decode instead of proceeding on a truncated file.
findings.failure_mode: fputc may buffer bytes that only fail to flush at fclose (disk full/quota). With the return discarded, the code proceeds to decode a truncated Group 4 spool as if complete, producing a corrupted image or a downstream decode error rather than a clean failure.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether write integrity matters for this coder; if so, check fclose (and consider fflush before close) and error out on failure.

findings.title: CloneImageInfo return dereferenced without NULL check (latent)
findings.invariant: A pointer returned by an allocator must be NULL-checked before it is dereferenced (SetImageInfoBlob and the subsequent read_info->filename/size/density/orientation writes).
findings.failure_mode: If CloneImageInfo returned NULL under memory pressure, SetImageInfoBlob(NULL,...) or read_info->filename would dereference NULL and crash. In stock ImageMagick CloneImageInfo aborts internally on OOM rather than returning NULL, so this is latent and depends on the library contract.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the CloneImageInfo contract for the target ImageMagick version — does it ever return NULL, or does it fatally abort?

audit_checklist: In the group4/CCITT decoder reached via ReadImage, confirm read_info->size (attacker WxH) cannot drive an unbounded or integer-overflowing allocation; clamp declared dimensions in ReadCALSImage.
Replace the (void) sscanf(...) casts with explicit conversion-count checks so malformed records are rejected rather than silently defaulted.
Verify ThrowImageException at line 117 releases the temp-file reservation/descriptor and destroys the open Image on the fdopen-failure path.
Check fclose (and fflush) of the spool file so a failed final flush aborts instead of decoding a truncated stream.
Confirm CloneImageInfo/AcquireImage cannot return NULL in the deployed version, or add NULL guards before dereference.
Re-confirm header stays NUL-terminated (memset stays outside the loop, ReadBlob length stays 128, buffer stays >=129) so LocaleNCompare/sscanf remain in-bounds.
Validate that width/height/density defaults (0) are safe to hand to the downstream decoder when the corresponding records are absent.
